aliases: කළු කරිය / කළු කුකුල් මස් (Kalu Kariya / Kalu Kukul Mas)

romanized: කළු කරිය / කළු කුකුල් මස් (Kalu Kariya / Kalu Kukul Mas)

food_subgroup: Bone-in chicken in dark roasted coconut-spice gravy; cooked dish with sauce; 1 serving ~250g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry (kalu kariya). Closest parent would be a generic chicken curry concept. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sri Lankan dark roasted coconut chicken curry (kalu kariya) — closest parent match under poultry-based curry dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GDD is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a coconut-based poultry curry. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Sri Lankan kalu kariya specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry. Not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

preparation_method: dry-roasting of coconut and spices until almost black
slow simmering bone-in chicken in coconut milk and spice gravy

serving.description: 1 serving (~250g): bone-in chicken pieces ~150g + thick dark roasted coconut-spice sauce ~100g
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry is approximately 250g, comprising roughly 150g of bone-in chicken pieces and 100g of thick dark roasted coconut-spice gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry — Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Profile (2012) supplemented with USDA SR Legacy component calculation for roasted coconut, coconut milk, and spice contributions
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) provides the primary compositional anchor for Sri Lankan chicken curry dishes. USDA SR Legacy was used for component-level cross-validation of roasted coconut, coconut milk, and spice contributions. Per-100g values represent the combined dish (chicken + sauce) at approximately 60% chicken / 40% sauce ratio. Values are consistent with the input record and have been retained as verified.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Low-Carbohydrate Meat and Coconut-Based Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-carbohydrate mixed meat dishes and coconut-based preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry (kalu kariya) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~10 is estimated based on the very low available carbohydrate content (~3.2g per 100g; ~8g per 250g serving) and the established principle that high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate dishes have negligible glycaemic impact. GL per serving = (10 × 8) ÷ 100 = 0.8, rounded to 0.7 per input record. This estimate is consistent with analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~240mg per 100g; ~600mg per 250g serving (~30% of WHO 2000mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry to one serving and avoid adding extra salt, as the ~600mg sodium per serving represents approximately 30% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit; the dish is otherwise free of processed sodium additives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~10) and very low GL (~0.7 per serving); high protein (28g per serving) attenuates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycaemic index (~10), negligible glycaemic load (~0.7 per 250g serving), and high protein content (~28g per serving) which strongly attenuates postprandial glucose; fenugreek in the spice blend may provide marginal additional glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~5.5g per 100g; ~13.8g per serving) predominantly from coconut milk and dry-roasted coconut (lauric and myristic acids)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry in moderation due to its high saturated fat content (~13.8g per 250g serving, predominantly coconut-derived lauric and myristic acids); the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, coriander, cumin) provides modest offsetting benefit, but saturated fat intake should be monitored within daily limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut; anti-inflammatory spice compounds (turmeric, cumin, coriander)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high saturated fat content (~13.8g per serving from coconut milk and roasted coconut) is the primary concern for rheumatoid arthritis; however, the spice profile including turmeric, coriander, and cumin provides modest anti-inflammatory plant compounds that may partially offset this concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry (kalu kariya) is a traditional Sri Lankan bone-in chicken curry distinguished by its defining preparation technique: fresh or desiccated coconut is dry-roasted in a pan until almost black, then ground together with coriander, cumin, chilli, and other spices into a dark, intensely flavoured curry paste. This Maillard reaction-driven process creates a deep, complex, slightly bitter-sweet flavour profile that is entirely distinct from Indian coconut curries (which use pre-dried spice powders) or Malaysian coconut curries (which use fresh coconut without dark roasting). The finished curry is a very dark brown to near-black colour, thick, richly aromatic, and intensely savoury. Bone-in chicken pieces are slow-simmered in this roasted coconut-spice paste with coconut milk, curry leaf, rampe (pandan), lemongrass, and fenugreek until the sauce reduces to a thick, clinging gravy. Per 100g: 163 kcal, 11.2g protein, 9.6g fat (5.5g saturated, predominantly coconut-derived lauric and myristic acids), 3.2g carbohydrate, 240mg sodium. Per standard serving (~250g): ~408 kcal, ~28g protein, ~24g fat, ~8g carbohydrate, ~600mg sodium. GI ~10 (very low), GL ~0.7 per serving. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is widely prepared across both Sinhalese and Tamil communities in Sri Lanka and is a staple of Sri Lankan restaurant menus in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is high in protein and saturated fat, very low in carbohydrates, and has negligible glycaemic impact, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns when sodium intake is managed.
common_user_queries: Sri Lankan chicken curry vs Indian chicken curry
is Sri Lankan black curry good for diabetes
roasted coconut chicken curry nutrition facts
meal_context: festive or everyday Sri Lankan meal

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are derived from the Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Profile (2012) supplemented with USDA SR Legacy component calculation. No single approved-source entry exists for Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry as a named dish; values represent a well-supported component-level estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on the very low carbohydrate content of the dish. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry exists in a primary approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Sri Lankan Black Chicken Curry (kalu kariya) as a composite dish is not available in the Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level; component-level fatty acid data for coconut milk and chicken exist but dish-level integration is not published. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the combined dish (chicken + thick sauce) is not reported in the Sri Lanka MRI FCP (2012) or USDA SR Legacy at this dish level. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; confirmed by USDA SR Legacy component review). This is a Sri Lankan dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Hindi) do not apply. Tamil alias is provided as the dish is also prepared in Tamil communities in Sri Lanka and among the Sri Lankan Tamil diaspora in Singapore. Sinhala script alias is provided for completeness. The FoodEx2 code A0GDD is used as the closest defensible parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for kalu kariya specifically.
